in

signals.

  At  the  University  at  Buffalo,  I  plan  to  resolve  a  central  puzzle  in  contemporary  credit  risk  
modeling,
 
namely,
 
whether

to

AI

financial
 
environments.

AI

interpretable
 
prediction,
 
enabling
 
fair
 
and
 
transparent
 
decision-making
 
across
 
financial
 
domains.
 
Using
 
a
 
combination
 
of
 
structured
 
borrower
 
data
 
and
 
unstructured
 
financial
 
text
 
from
 
publicly
 
available
 
sources,
 
such
 
as
 
FRED,

Bank,
 
Kaggle,
 
and
 
potential
 
industry
 
collaborations,
 
I
 
will
 
test
 
whether
 
automatically
 
learned,
 
interpretable
 
representations
 
can
 
match
 
or
 
outperform
 
conventional
 
black-box
 
systems.

text:
My  proposed  methodology  will  combine  tabular  text  fusion  models,  autoencoder-based  feature  
discovery,
 
fairness
 
metrics,
 
and
 
inherently
 
interpretable
 
predictive
 
architectures,
 
evaluated
 
through
 
AUC,
 
Gini,
 
KS,
 
and
 
XAI
 
tools
 
such
 
as
 
SHAP
 
and
 
attention
 
visualization.
 
The
 
novelty
 
lies
 
in
 
embedding
 
interpretability
 
directly
 
into
 
the
 
model’s
 
internal
 
representation,
 
rather
 
than
 
treating
 
it
 
as
 
an
 
add-on,
 
thus
 
offering
 
a
 
transparent
 
and
 
robust
 
framework
 
for
 
credit
 
scoring.
 
Theoretically,
 
this
 
work
 
contributes
 
to
 
Responsible
 
AI
 
by
 
challenging
 
the
 
assumption
 
that
 
accuracy
 
and
 
explainability
 
are
 
fundamentally
 
incompatible,
 
and
 
by
 
proposing
 
a
 
representation-learning
 
framework
 
that
 
can
 
generalize
 
to
 
other
 
risk-sensitive
 
domains.
